rules.title: Rule of Cool
rules.shortVersion: Creative and interesting ideas deserve a fair chance.
rules.fullExplanation: The rules matter, but memorable roleplay, clever solutions, and dramatic moments matter too. The DM may allow an idea, ask for a roll, add a cost, or create a consequence.
rules.whyTheTableUsesIt: It encourages players to describe interesting actions instead of only pressing rules buttons.
rules.exampleInPlay: A player tries to swing from a chandelier, kick an enemy, and land behind cover. The DM decides what roll is needed and what can go wrong.
rules.exceptions: It does not erase danger, invalidate another character, or guarantee success.

rules.title: Roleplay First, Then Roll
rules.shortVersion: Say what your character tries to do before asking for a roll.
rules.fullExplanation: Players describe the action in plain language. The DM decides whether it works, requires a roll, or has a consequence.
rules.whyTheTableUsesIt: It keeps the focus on the shared fiction instead of turning every moment into a button on a sheet.
rules.exampleInPlay: Say "I want to talk the guard into letting us through" instead of "I roll Persuasion."
rules.exceptions: Fast repeated checks may be abbreviated once everyone understands the scene.

rules.title: Stay With the Party
rules.shortVersion: The party should generally stay together.
rules.fullExplanation: Splitting up slows the game, separates players from the action, and can leave a character in danger without support. Temporary splits should be discussed.
rules.whyTheTableUsesIt: It helps keep a larger group engaged and easier to run.
rules.exampleInPlay: Scouting one hallway ahead may be fine. Leaving for a separate solo mission should be discussed first.
rules.exceptions: The DM may call for a temporary split when the story needs it.

rules.title: Six-Player Streamlining
rules.shortVersion: Some rulings may exist to keep a six-person group moving.
rules.fullExplanation: The DM has said some rulings were created, and more may emerge, to keep a six-person group moving. Specific rules should be logged here once named.
rules.whyTheTableUsesIt: Larger groups need clearer pacing tools.
rules.exampleInPlay: A future turn timing guideline or simplified procedure can be added here.
rules.exceptions: Not active until the DM records the exact rule.
